flashcards.back: A maximal connected subgraph of an undirected graph. Every pair of vertices in the component has a path between them, and no additional vertex can be included without breaking this property.

flashcards.front: How do you find all connected components of a graph?
flashcards.back: Iterate through all vertices. For each unvisited vertex, run DFS or BFS -- all vertices reached form one connected component. Increment the component counter. Repeat until all vertices are visited.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between connected components and edges?
flashcards.back: A graph with V vertices and C connected components has at least V - C edges (each component needs at least a spanning tree). A connected graph (C=1) needs at least V-1 edges.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between connected and strongly connected?
flashcards.back: Connected applies to undirected graphs (path between every pair). Strongly connected applies to directed graphs (directed path from every vertex to every other vertex).

flashcards.front: Can a single isolated vertex be a connected component?
flashcards.back: Yes. An isolated vertex (with no edges) forms a connected component by itself. A graph with n isolated vertices has n connected components.

flashcards.front: Time complexity of finding connected components?
flashcards.back: O(V + E) using DFS or BFS with an adjacency list. Each vertex and each edge is examined exactly once across all component-finding calls.
